title: Batch Certification vs Batch Release: The Difference
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A QP's guide to batch certification vs release: what each act means under Annex 16, who is accountable, and why certification must always come before release.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette over deep navy, lit by soft natural light. In sharp foreground focus, a gloved hand sets a fountain pen against a pristine batch certification register beside a sealed, labelled medicine carton waiting in quarantine; a single subtle green status indicator glows on a nearby rack. Behind, gently blurred with a shallow depth of field, rows of finished-product cartons sit on stainless-steel shelving bathed in cyan light, implying the moment a batch passes from quarantine into release. The mood is precise, authoritative and premium, with clean reflective surfaces and fine detail on the documentation. 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: Can the same person certify and release a batch?
faqs.a: They can, but the two acts remain distinct even when performed by one individual. Certification is the QP's personal legal confirmation under Annex 16 that the batch meets its marketing authorisation and GMP, while release is the operational step of moving certified stock into saleable status. In many organisations release is handled by trained warehouse or logistics staff once the QP has certified and recorded the batch, which is perfectly acceptable provided the system prevents release before certification.

faqs.q: Is a batch legally on the market once it is released but not yet certified?
faqs.a: No. Under EU GMP and Annex 16 a batch cannot be lawfully placed on the market until a Qualified Person has certified it and entered it in the certification register. A stock-system status of "released" carries no regulatory weight on its own. Shipping product that has been released operationally but never certified by a QP is a serious GMP breach and a predictable inspection finding.

faqs.q: Who certifies imported or contract-manufactured batches?
faqs.a: For product imported into the UK, a UK QP named on the relevant Manufacturer's/Importer's Authorisation must certify each batch before it enters the supply chain. Where manufacture and testing span several sites, each party may confirm their stage, but a single QP takes ultimate responsibility for the final certification. The full chain should be defined in a written technical agreement so accountability is unambiguous if the MHRA asks.
